dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a sick person call at [CITY].
Hello, I'm Medic Taylor with the EMS team. What led you or your family to call 911 today?
Your daughter told us you've had altered mental status and vomiting off and on throughout the day, which started earlier today, with malaise.
You are now alert and oriented to person, place, time, and events, with no pain reported.
However, you were initially found with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 11.
During initial assessment, you responded promptly to verbal and physical stimuli.
We note your past medical history includes insulin-dependent diabetes, primary hypertension, hepatitis C, and bipolar disorder as discussed with your daughter.
Your initial blood pressure is 189 over 128, with a pulse of 128 and respiratory rate of 14.
We found your blood glucose level to be 326, which is hyperglycemic.
The EKG shows a rhythm of sinus tachycardia.
An IV has been started in your left forearm in an extremity vein.
You are now on a 4-lead ECG monitor per medical protocol for altered mental status.
Airway is patent, clear, and maintained fully by you with no sounds of obstruction.
Breathing is at a rate of 10 to 16 breaths per minute and depth is normal, with no flaring.
Skin is warm and dry with good peripheral pulses and no signs of impaired circulation.
Blood pressure remains elevated and pulse is consistently high.
You open your eyes spontaneously without external stimulation.
Your answers indicate you are alert and oriented to person, time, place, and events (A&O x 4).
You respond appropriately to all commands and follow directions.
Your skin is warm, dry, with no petechiae, rashes, or bite marks noted.
No trauma or deformity noted; pupils appear equal and reactive.
HEENT is atraumatic with no drainage or obstruction identified.
Chest has symmetrical expansion with clear and equal bilateral breath sounds.
No signs of wheezing, crackles, or other adventitious sounds.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, with no guarding or masses on examination.
Evaluating the extremities, pulses are strong with intact sensation and movement.
No deformities or angulation found.
Your daughter reported that you had episodes of vomiting throughout the day, leading to a concern for diabetic-related complications.
No specific time of the last meal, but it is estimated to be earlier in the day.
Given altered mental status, vomiting, fever, and malaise as field impressions, sepsis is suspected.
You do not report any pain or discomfort.
Your latest readings show a blood pressure of 198 over 133, pulse 126, respiratory rate of 11, and oxygen saturation of 98%.
Another check shows a blood pressure of 195 over 127, pulse of 122, respiratory rate of 16, and oxygen saturation is at 99%.
Your latest vitals show a pulse of 124, blood pressure remains at 195 over 127, with a respiratory rate of 10 and oxygen saturation at 100%.
No signs of rectal bleeding or urinary urgency reported.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive with no evidence of trauma.
No signs of neck masses, stiffness, or palpable nodes.
No signs of midline deviation or nasal obstruction.
The patient denies any urinary urgency or suprapubic discomfort.
No signs of pelvic pain or instability.
Heart sounds are normal rhythm and strength with no murmurs.
No abnormal chest movement or instability detected.
EKG strips are being continuously monitored.
We are preparing for transport to [COMPANY_NAME] per protocol for altered mental status.
Stretcher is being loaded and secured with five safety straps.
The hospital is approximately twelve minutes away, and we are navigating with route scoping.
Vitals remain consistent with earlier readings at 195 over 127, pulse 124, and SpO2 100%.
The IV line remains patent at the established site.
You were found non-responsive initially but are now alert, with all secondary exams found unremarkable.
The patient is now secured, and all equipment is in place for transport.
Destination has been confirmed by dispatch and the receiving emergency department.
We have transmitted the patient care record including vital signs and interventions.
Transport time, patient status, and monitoring are documented and communicated to ER staff.
No signs of dehydration observed at this time.
All systems verified, and we are now departing with the patient.
Lights and sirens are on, and we are en route safely.
We are approaching MRMC with all systems activated for safe arrival.
Each breath is being monitored and no changes in pattern noted prior to departure.
Peripheral pulses are good, skin is warm, and oxygen saturation remains at 100%.
IV flow remains unimpeded, and no issues with the infusion noted.
No medications administered at this time due to freedom from pain or distress.
Oxygen tanks and IV pole are secured for transport.
All protocol steps completed, patient is ready for hand-off at arrival.
The cabin temperature is set to 69 degrees Fahrenheit with normal humidity.
Patient is informed regarding expected arrival and care on arrival.
Receiving RN is aware and prepared for our patient's arrival.
You are currently on Lantus, Metoprolol, trazodone, and several other medications.
Paramedic notes, vitals, and EKG strips are documented for ER access.
Any changes during transport will be relayed to hospital staff.
Vehicle has safely departed and is en route with continuous monitoring.
Expected arrival at hospital is within 12 minutes based on current traffic.
4-lead EKG monitoring is ongoing and transmitting in real time.
Blood pressure is being recorded and monitored for hospital access.
Emergency room staff is ready for patient intake and care.
ER nurse has been notified and is preparing for patient arrival.
Crew is conducting routine checks consistent with protocol.
Any drug allergies of concern?
No current changes in known allergies.
No new signs of IV complications found.
File has been shared for hospital access and available upon arrival.
Baseline vitals and prescribed medications are verified and documented.
Patient remains stable with findings matching established profile.
EMS status is logged and ready for departure details.
Timeline and notes have been documented for review at hospital.
Patient data is accessible and complete in hospital systems.
Command center confirms complete and successful transport process.
Patient transportation is complete, and all data is in place.
Patient remains under established care protocols for hospital handoff.
No pending complaints or gaps in care.
Full report has been uploaded to cloud and ER systems.
Patient status remains stable with no signs of decline.
Crew actions align with established procedures.
Any confusion noted during the process is acknowledged.
Transport process follows the final green checklist status.
Protocol for altered mental status is complete and summarized.
Amber lights remain active due to safety, although weather is favorable.
All required steps of protocol have been completed.
Post-transport cleaning is scheduled for unit hygiene.
No backup nurse or physician contacted during the transport.
